rules: explain one idea at a time

lessons.title: Writing your first program
lessons.learning_goal: Write and execute a simple Python script that shows text on the screen using the print() function.

lesson_chunks.goal: Explain what Python and programming are, from zero.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: A computer cannot think for itself — it only does exactly what you tell it. Telling a computer what to do, step by step, is called programming. Python is a programming language: a set of words and rules for writing those instructions. People like Python because it reads almost like plain English, which makes it a great first language to learn.
lesson_chunks.check_question: Is Python a language we use to give a computer instructions? (yes / no)

common_wrong_answers.hint: Actually yes — Python is exactly that: a language for giving a computer instructions.

lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Exactly — Python is how we give the computer instructions.
lesson_chunks.hint: Python is the language we write our instructions in — so, yes.
source_refs: PY101 Week 1, Writing your first program

lesson_chunks.goal: Introduce the ideas of a command, a program, and running top to bottom.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: In Python you write instructions, and each single instruction is called a command. You normally write one command per line. The computer carries them out in order, starting at the top and moving down, one line at a time. A group of commands saved together is called a program.
lesson_chunks.check_question: What do we call a single instruction you give the computer in Python?
lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Right — one instruction is a command, and the computer runs them top to bottom.
lesson_chunks.hint: Another word for one instruction; it starts with the letter 'c'.
source_refs: PY101 Week 1, Writing your first program

lesson_chunks.goal: Introduce print() as the first command, which shows text on screen.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: Your very first command is print(). It tells the computer to show something on the screen. You write the word print, then a pair of parentheses ( ), and the message you want to show goes inside those parentheses.
lesson_chunks.check_question: Which command tells the computer to show text on the screen?

common_wrong_answers.hint: Good instinct, but in Python the command is simply called print.

lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Yes — print() is the command that shows things on the screen.
lesson_chunks.hint: It's the word you write just before the parentheses: ____( ).
source_refs: PY101 Week 1, Writing your first program

lesson_chunks.goal: Introduce strings: the message goes inside quotation marks.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: The message you want to show goes inside quotation marks. A piece of text inside quotes is called a string. The quotes tell Python where your text starts and where it ends.
lesson_chunks.check_question: What does print("Hi") show on the screen?
lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Right — it shows the text that is inside the quotes.
lesson_chunks.hint: Python shows exactly what is written between the quotation marks.
source_refs: PY101 Week 1, Writing your first program

lesson_chunks.goal: Clarify that quotation marks never appear in the output.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: The quotation marks are only markers for Python. They tell it where the text begins and ends, but they themselves never appear in what is shown on the screen.
lesson_chunks.check_question: Do the quotation marks appear on the screen when the program runs? (yes / no)

common_wrong_answers.hint: Actually no — look at the example: print("Python") shows Python with no quotes. The quotes are only markers.

lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Correct — the quotes are just markers and are never shown.
lesson_chunks.hint: Look at the example: the quotes are gone in what gets shown.
source_refs: PY101 Week 1, Writing your first program

lesson_chunks.goal: Have the student write a full print() command.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: Now you can write a full command: write print, then parentheses, and put your text in quotes inside the parentheses. That single line is a complete instruction for the computer.
lesson_chunks.check_question: Write a command that shows the word:  Python
lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: 🎉 That's a real line of Python!
lesson_chunks.hint: Write print, then ("Python") — your text in quotes inside the parentheses.
source_refs: PY101 Week 1, Writing your first program

lesson_chunks.goal: Explain what it means to run (execute) the program.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: Writing the command is the first step. To see it work, you run the program, which is also called executing it. When you run it, Python reads your command and shows the text on the screen.
lesson_chunks.check_question: When you run print("Hello"), what appears on the screen?
lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Exactly — running the command shows the text on the screen.
lesson_chunks.hint: Running it shows the text inside the quotes — without the quotes.
source_refs: PY101 Week 1, Writing your first program

lesson_chunks.goal: Combine writing and running into one complete first script.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: You now know the whole flow: write a print command with your message in quotes, then run the program to see the output. Putting those two steps together is your first complete Python script.
lesson_chunks.check_question: Write a command that, when you run it, shows:  I love Python
lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: 🎉 You can write and run a Python script — nicely done!
lesson_chunks.hint: Use print(...) with your whole message in quotes: print("I love Python").
source_refs: PY101 Week 1, Writing your first program

common_mistakes.remediation: Remind that print is a command that needs (): the message goes inside them.

common_mistakes.description: Student writes print(Hello) without quotes around the text.
common_mistakes.remediation: Text must be inside quotes so Python treats it as a string.

common_mistakes.description: Student expects the quotation marks to appear on screen.
common_mistakes.remediation: Show that the quotes are markers and are not part of the output.

common_mistakes.description: Student writes Print or prnt; Python is case-sensitive.
common_mistakes.remediation: Point out the exact spelling: lowercase print.

lessons.title: Unlocking print() power-ups
lessons.learning_goal: Use print() to show multiple items on one line and to make blank lines.

lesson_chunks.goal: Show that commas let print() display several items.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: You can give print() more than one thing to show at once by separating them with commas. It displays them on the same line, in order.
lesson_chunks.check_question: How do you separate several items inside one print() so it shows them all?

common_wrong_answers.hint: Inside print(), you separate multiple items with commas.

lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Yes — commas let one print() show several items.
lesson_chunks.hint: It's the punctuation mark you also use to separate items in a list.

lesson_chunks.goal: Explain the automatic single space between comma-separated items.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: When you separate items with commas, print() automatically puts one space between them. You do not need to add those spaces yourself.
lesson_chunks.check_question: With commas, how many spaces does print() put between the items — zero, one, or two?

common_wrong_answers.hint: Commas add exactly one space automatically between items.

lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Right — commas add exactly one space.
lesson_chunks.hint: It adds just a single space between each item.

lesson_chunks.goal: Show that an empty print() makes a blank line.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: Calling print() with nothing inside the parentheses prints an empty line. It is a simple way to add space between other lines of output.
lesson_chunks.check_question: What does an empty print() — with nothing inside — display?
lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Exactly — empty print() prints a blank line.
lesson_chunks.hint: With nothing to show, it just leaves an empty line.

lesson_chunks.goal: Combine multi-item printing into one line of code.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: Putting it together: separate items with commas to show them on one line, and use an empty print() for a blank line. These small tricks shape your output.
lesson_chunks.check_question: Write one print() that shows the two words:  good morning
lesson_chunks.hint: Use print() with the two words in quotes, separated by a comma.

common_mistakes.description: Student adds spaces manually instead of relying on commas.
common_mistakes.remediation: Note that commas already add one space between items.

lessons.title: Introduction to variables
lessons.learning_goal: Store a value in a variable and use it by name.

lesson_chunks.goal: Introduce a variable as a named store, created with =.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: A variable is a name that stores a value so you can use it later. You create one with an equals sign: the name on the left, the value on the right. For example, age = 10 stores the number 10 in a name called age.
lesson_chunks.check_question: In  age = 10 , what goes on the LEFT of the equals sign — the name or the value?

common_wrong_answers.hint: The name goes on the left, the value on the right: name = value.

lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Right — the name goes on the left of the equals sign.
lesson_chunks.hint: You name the box first, then put a value in it: name = value.

lesson_chunks.goal: Use a variable by writing its name.
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: Once a variable holds a value, you use it by writing its name. Writing the name gives you back what is stored. For example, after age = 10, writing print(age) shows 10.
lesson_chunks.check_question: After  age = 10 , what does print(age) show?

common_wrong_answers.hint: Without quotes, print(age) shows what's stored in age — the number 10.

lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Yes — print(age) shows the stored value, 10.
lesson_chunks.hint: It shows the value stored in age, not the word 'age'.

lesson_chunks.goal: Distinguish a variable name (no quotes) from text (quotes).
lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: Quotes matter. print(age) shows the value stored in the variable age, but print("age") shows the word age as plain text. No quotes means the variable's value; quotes mean the literal text.
lesson_chunks.simple_example:
age = 10
print(age)     shows:   10
print("age")   shows:   age
lesson_chunks.check_question: Does print("age") show the stored value, or the word age? (value / word)

common_wrong_answers.hint: With quotes it's literal text — so it shows the word age, not the stored value.

lesson_chunks.correct_feedback: Correct — quotes mean literal text, so it shows the word age.
lesson_chunks.hint: Quotes always mean 'the literal text', not the variable's value.

lesson_chunks.approved_explanation: So: store a value with the equals sign (name on the left), use it by writing its name without quotes, and remember quotes always mean literal text. Variables let your program remember and reuse information.
lesson_chunks.check_question: Write code that stores the number 5 in a variable called count.

common_wrong_answers.hint: The name goes on the LEFT of the equals sign: count = 5.

lesson_chunks.hint: Put the name first, then =, then the value: name = value.

common_mistakes.description: Student writes value = name instead of name = value.
common_mistakes.remediation: The variable name goes on the left of the equals sign.

common_mistakes.description: Student quotes a variable name and prints the word instead of the value.
common_mistakes.remediation: No quotes to use the variable's value; quotes mean literal text.
